surface.name: PayPal REST API get-started path through first authenticated sandbox API response
surface.selection_basis: PayPal's official REST API landing page is titled Get started with PayPal REST APIs and orders credentials, access token, then API calls. The Authentication guide ends at the token; PayPal's API Requests guide continues with authenticated REST calls using Authorization: Bearer ACCESS-TOKEN. Its sample request is a GET of sandbox invoices. First success is that authenticated API response, not the token alone.

alternatives_considered.name: REST APIs in Postman without a PayPal Developer account
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It delegates credentials and requests to a Postman workspace rather than measuring PayPal's own account, app, and credential path.

alternatives_considered.name: PayPal Checkout integration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a product-specific Node.js front-end and back-end build that starts after credentials and introduces payment approval and capture.

alternatives_considered.name: Orders v2 API checkout flow
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires product, intent, payment source, buyer approval, and capture choices after obtaining an access token.

alternatives_considered.name: Complete Payments Platform
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a partner platform path with business evaluation, seller onboarding, and approval requirements.

entry_point.developer_intent: Obtain PayPal sandbox REST credentials, request an OAuth access token, and complete the first authenticated PayPal REST API call.
assumed_prior_state: The developer does not yet have a PayPal Developer Dashboard session or copied sandbox REST app credentials.
The developer is testing a US integration, so a PayPal Developer account is sufficient for this sandbox token path.
The cURL variant is used because it is the first implementation tab on the official get-started page.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Use the sandbox access token to call a PayPal REST API, as shown by the API Requests sample GET invoices request.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: PayPal's sandbox REST API accepts the bearer access token and returns an HTTP success response for GET /v1/invoicing/invoices.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The invoices list request returns an HTTP 2xx JSON response from https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com while authenticated with the bearer token.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Get started documents getting an access token, then using it for API calls. API Requests shows combining method, URL, resource URI, and Authorization: Bearer ACCESS-TOKEN, and samples GET https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com/v1/invoicing/invoices.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: An access token only proves credential exchange. PayPal's own get-started path continues into authenticated API calls; the API Requests sample GET invoices call is the earliest documented authenticated REST request after the token and does not require constructing an Orders checkout payload.

prerequisites.requirement: A PayPal Developer account for testing a US integration.
A PayPal Business account if testing outside the US or later going live.
cURL or Postman; this record uses cURL.
HTTPS access to the PayPal Developer Dashboard and api-m.sandbox.paypal.com.

primary_path.action: Open Get started with PayPal REST APIs.
details: The page covers shared setup for orders, payments, subscriptions, invoicing, and disputes.
primary_path.input: Intent to call a PayPal REST API.
primary_path.output: Ordered credentials and OAuth token instructions.
primary_path.success_signal: Get client ID and client secret is the first numbered section.

details: The documentation directs new developers to log in or sign up.
primary_path.input: PayPal account or intent to create one.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer reaches a PayPal login or account-creation screen.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The linked account flow can introduce region, identity, email, phone, or business-information screens that this API guide does not enumerate.

primary_path.action: Log in to PayPal or complete sign-up, then reach the Developer Dashboard.
details: A PayPal Developer account can test US integrations.
A Business account is required for testing outside the US or going live.
primary_path.input: Eligible PayPal identity and required account details.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Account creation, verification, eligibility, or risk checks are not decomposed in the developer guide.

primary_path.output: REST API apps and credentials view.
primary_path.success_signal: The Default Application or Create App action is visible.

primary_path.action: Use the Default Application in the REST API apps section.
details: New accounts include a Default Application.
Creating a new project is optional and uses Create App.
primary_path.success_signal: The application's client ID and client secret controls are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the default application is absent, the guide says to select Create App but does not enumerate that form.

primary_path.action: Copy the app's client ID and client secret.
details: The client ID identifies the app.
The client secret authenticates the client ID and must be kept safe.
primary_path.success_signal: Both credential values are available for the OAuth request.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A hidden secret may need to be revealed in the live interface, but the guide only says to copy it.

primary_path.action: Select the cURL example in Get access token.
details: Postman is the peer documented implementation.
primary_path.success_signal: The POST request to /v1/oauth2/token is visible.

primary_path.action: Replace CLIENT_ID and CLIENT_SECRET in the cURL command with the sandbox app credentials.
details: The command uses HTTP Basic authentication through -u CLIENT_ID:CLIENT_SECRET.
The guide notes that CLIENT_ID:CLIENT_SECRET is Base64-encoded before it is sent.
The request content type is application/x-www-form-urlencoded and the body is grant_type=client_credentials.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Swapped, incomplete, or live-environment credentials will not authenticate against the sandbox endpoint.

primary_path.action: Send the OAuth token request to PayPal's sandbox.
details: POST https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com/v1/oauth2/token.
Include client credentials, the form content type, and client_credentials grant.
primary_path.input: Credentialed cURL command and network access.
primary_path.success_signal: PayPal returns a JSON token response rather than an authentication error.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Authentication, endpoint, encoding, network, or environment mismatch can return a non-success HTTP response.

primary_path.action: Send the OAuth token request and capture the returned sandbox access token.
details: Confirm access_token is present.
Confirm token_type is Bearer.
Record expires_in because expired tokens must be replaced by calling the token endpoint again.
primary_path.success_signal: The JSON response includes access_token, token_type Bearer, and expires_in.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If no token is returned, compare the request with the documented credentials, sandbox URL, and client-credentials grant.

primary_path.action: Send the documented sample GET invoices request to the PayPal sandbox REST host with the bearer token.
details: PayPal's API Requests guide samples: GET https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com/v1/invoicing/invoices with Content-Type application/json and Authorization: Bearer ACCESS-TOKEN.
Optional query parameters such as page and page_size may be included as in the sample.
primary_path.input: Sandbox access token from the OAuth response
primary_path.success_signal: PayPal returns an HTTP 2xx JSON response for the invoices resource.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing, expired, or malformed bearer tokens produce authorization errors instead of invoice data.

primary_path.action: Confirm the authenticated REST response body from the sandbox invoices request.
details: An empty invoice list is still a successful authenticated API response when HTTP status is success.
primary_path.output: Verified first authenticated PayPal API success
primary_path.success_signal: The response demonstrates authorized access to a PayPal REST resource.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: HTTP 401 or 403 means the token was not accepted for API access.

branches.condition: The developer uses PayPal's Postman collection.
branches.path: Open the official Postman guide and workspace, use its supplied test credentials and requests, and try REST APIs without a PayPal Developer account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes PayPal Dashboard account and app-credential steps but adds Postman account, workspace, environment, and collection interactions.

branches.condition: The developer needs a distinct app rather than Default Application.
branches.path: Select Create App, supply the app's requested project information, create it, then copy its sandbox client ID and secret.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an app-creation form before token acquisition.

branches.condition: The developer chooses Postman for OAuth while still using their own app credentials.
branches.path: Select the Postman tab, configure its variables or authorization with client ID and secret, send the token request, and inspect the response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the CLI command with Postman UI steps.

branches.condition: The next goal is a product API call.
branches.path: Choose an API family and endpoint, put Authorization: Bearer ACCESS-TOKEN in the header, provide any required headers and body, send the request, and inspect its HTTP and JSON response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a product-specific payload and terminal outcome that cannot be selected from the company name alone.

branches.condition: The integration is tested outside the US or moved to production.
branches.path: Obtain a PayPal Business account and use live credentials and https://api-m.paypal.com when the integration is ready.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds business-account, live-app, eligibility, and production-readiness gates.

friction_gates.description: The documented own-credentials route requires a PayPal Developer account for US sandbox testing.
The guide routes through PayPal login or signup but does not enumerate the account-verification flow.
A PayPal Business account is required to test outside the US or go live.
A new account can use Default Application or create a separate app.
Both sandbox client ID and secret are required, and the secret must be protected.
The guide offers cURL and Postman token-request variants.
Access tokens expire, so a later call may require another token request.
A valid sandbox bearer access token is required in the Authorization header for REST API requests.

sources.title: Get Started with PayPal REST APIs
sections_used: Get client ID and client secret

uncertainties.question: What exact screens, fields, verification challenges, and eligibility checks occur during PayPal signup for a new developer?
uncertainties.impact: The account gate can contain additional pages before Apps & Credentials.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The REST API guide links to log in or sign up but does not enumerate the current PayPal account flow.

uncertainties.question: Does the current Dashboard require a separate click to reveal the client secret?
uncertainties.impact: It can add one credential-access action.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The documentation says Copy the client ID and client secret but does not describe secret masking or reveal controls.

uncertainties.question: Which product API should follow token acquisition?
uncertainties.impact: It changes request schema, permissions, actors, and observable first business result.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The get-started page serves orders, payments, subscriptions, invoicing, and disputes without selecting one.

excluded_after_success: Selecting and calling a product-specific REST API.
Retrieving buyer and seller sandbox account credentials.
Signing in to personal and business sandbox accounts.
Creating, approving, and capturing a payment order.
Webhooks, idempotency, rate limits, and negative testing.
PayPal Business account setup and production go-live.
